---
title: "[Punchy Title]: [The Concept or Tension at Stake]"
subtitle: "[A short, human framing line] — a COMP 1150 case study"
author: "Brendan Shea, PhD"
date: last-modified
# Format, bibliography, and styling are inherited from _quarto.yml
# (HTML + DOCX, refs.bib, _case-styles.scss). Override here only if a
# single case needs something special.
---

<!--
====================================================================
COMP 1150 CASE STUDY TEMPLATE  (Summer 2026)
Read case_study_style_guide.md before drafting. Quick reminders:

  - ~3,000 words of core prose (The Case + the argument dialogue);
    ~5,000 all-in once context boxes, figure, questions, refs are added.
  - Pairs with exactly one notebook (see course_outline.md table).
  - Two jobs, both required:
      (1) explain & APPLY some concept(s) from the paired notebook;
      (2) open a real history / ethics / current-practice debate that
          can spark discussion — not "recall the right answer."
  - The intellectual spine is "The Argument ___ Started": a connected
    dialogue of named .argument blocks, each answering the prior one.
    This is where the RCTC critical-thinking outcome lives.
  - Attribution: Brendan Shea, PhD. Never the college.
  - Delete every HTML comment before publishing.

Custom callout classes used below (.case-at-a-glance, .context-box,
.argument) need styling. A starter _case-styles.scss is referenced in
the style guide; until it exists they render as plain divs, which is
fine for drafting.
====================================================================
-->

::: {.case-at-a-glance}
- **Who:** [The named people / system / organization at the center. Concrete.]
- **What:** [One sentence: the event, decision, or artifact and what went wrong or was at stake.]
- **Where / When:** [Place(s) and dates. Be specific enough to ground the story.]
- **Why it matters:** [Why this is *the* canonical example of the tension. Name the two
  forces that collide using [key-term]{.key-term} spans — e.g.
  [correctness]{.key-term} vs. [delivery pressure]{.key-term}.]
- **Pairs with:** [Notebook N — Title] · **Concepts applied:** [list the
  notebook concepts this case makes students actually use]
:::

## The Case

<!--
The narrative. ~1,200–1,500 words. Tell it like the Dax case: real
people doing recognizable things, present-tense stakes, no editorializing
yet. The reader should care before any argument starts. Plant — but do
not yet resolve — the technical facts the later arguments will turn on.

Use .context-box callouts to teach the notebook concept exactly when the
story needs it, so the CS content is load-bearing, not bolted on. Bold
new technical terms and mark them with [term]{.key-term} for the glossary
/ styling.
-->

[Open in a scene. Establish the people and the system. Build to the
decision or failure.]

::: {.context-box}
**[The concept the paired notebook teaches, explained here because the story needs it.]**
[2–5 sentences. Define each [technical term]{.key-term} in plain language.
Tie it to what the reader just saw happen. This is the bridge between the
notebook's mechanics and the case's stakes — e.g. for NB 4, what a
[race condition]{.key-term} is and why a reused [flag variable]{.key-term}
in the control flow made it possible.]
:::

[Continue the narrative. What was tried, who objected, what the
institution did. End the section on the unresolved human question — not a
verdict. State plainly that the *outcome* is not the interesting part;
*whose call it was / what we should have known / what we owed* is.]

## The Argument [X] Started

<!--
The spine of the case study. ~1,500–1,800 words.

A *connected* dialogue: each .argument block answers the one before it.
Reconstruct real positions (engineers, historians, ethicists, courts,
companies) — steelman every side. Aim for 3–5 linked moves:

  1. The clean opening argument (often the "obvious" modern view).
  2. The strongest reply that attacks ONE shared premise.
  3. A counter that repairs or redirects.
  4. (optional) A reframing move that changes the question.
  5. The unresolved / empirical turn — where the debate actually rests
     today, with current-practice evidence.

Between blocks, write the connective prose that says which premise is
under attack and why. Use .context-box for the CS/historical background
a reader needs to evaluate a move. End not at an answer but a sharper
question.
-->

### [The opening argument — a short heading naming the move]

[1–2 paragraphs of setup: whose position this is and in their own words
(quote + @citekey where possible).]

::: {.argument}
**The [Name] Argument**

1. [Premise 1.]
2. [Premise 2 — usually the one the reply will attack.]
3. Therefore, [conclusion].
:::

[Say which premise carries the weight, and hand off to the reply.]

::: {.context-box}
**[Background concept needed to judge this argument.]**
[Define [terms]{.key-term}. Keep it tight. Cite history/standards with
@citekey where relevant — e.g. when a standard or technique was actually
new, so the reader judges the actors by what they could have known.]
:::

### [The reply — names the premise it denies]

[Setup prose: this side does not deny premise 1; it denies premise 2,
because…]

::: {.argument}
**The [Name] Reply**

1. [Premise.]
2. [Premise.]
3. [Premise.]
4. Therefore, [conclusion].
:::

[Name the soft spot in the reply — the move the next block exploits.]

### [The counter]

::: {.argument}
**The [Name] Objection**

1. [Premise.]
2. [Premise.]
3. Therefore, [conclusion].
:::

<!-- Add a reframing block and/or the empirical-turn block as the case
demands. Keep each tied to the one before. -->

### [The argument that hasn't ended: the current-practice turn]

[Where does this rest *today*? Use current evidence: a recent incident,
a standard that did/didn't get adopted, what industry actually does now
vs. what the tidy lesson says. Close on the sharper question, not a
resolution.]

<!--
OPTIONAL FIGURE. Use a {dot} graphviz block when a decision/process is
the cleanest way to show a position (cf. the Arnold–Menzel diagram).
All figures generated by code, fully labeled, with a fig-cap that
explains how to read it. Delete this block if unused.
-->

```{dot}
//| label: fig-decision
//| fig-cap: "[One sentence saying what the diagram shows and how to read it — the gold node is the pivot, the green node is the endpoint, etc. Cite the source it is adapted from.]"
digraph G {
  rankdir=TB;
  node [shape=box, style="rounded,filled", fillcolor="#eef3f8",
        fontname="Helvetica", fontsize=11, margin="0.18,0.10"];
  edge [fontname="Helvetica", fontsize=10];

  start [label="[Starting situation]", fillcolor="#dde8f0"];
  pivot [label="[The decisive question]", shape=diamond, fillcolor="#f3e9d8"];
  endp  [label="[The endpoint]", fillcolor="#e3efe1"];

  start -> pivot;
  pivot -> endp [label="  [condition]"];
}
```

## Discussion Questions

<!--
5 questions. RCTC critical-thinking outcome: students must evaluate
competing claims, not recall answers. At least one should make them
reconstruct two arguments and find the shared disputed premise. At least
one should put them inside the scenario as a decision-maker. At least
one should connect to a paired-notebook concept they can actually run
code on. No question should have a single correct answer.
-->

1. Reconstruct **The [X] Argument** and **The [Y] Reply** in your own words.
   Identify the one premise they actually disagree about, and say what kind
   of evidence — technical, historical, ethical — could settle it.
2. [A "this is not a contradiction / turned out fine ≠ was right" question,
   using a concept named in the case.]
3. [A question pressing on a generalization, standard, or trade-off the case
   used — when is it legitimate to apply it here?]
4. You are [a specific role inside the scenario] at the moment [the decision].
   Drawing on [a concept from NB N], what do you do, and what do you owe that
   the institution does not?
5. Apply [the framework / procedure / standard] to [a second, contrasting
   real case]. Does it yield the outcome that case's people endorse? If not,
   is that a flaw in the framework, a difference between the cases, or
   evidence the debate is unresolved?

## Further Reading

<!-- 3–5 entries: the documentaries / primary reports / key essays /
authoritative journalism. Each with an @citekey that exists in refs.bib. -->

- [Primary source — report, paper, or documentary] [@citekey].
- [Key secondary analysis] [@citekey].
- [Accessible journalism or retrospective] [@citekey].

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
